The definition in the form is not clear and it can even be misleading. We believe that there should be clear and separated fields for the archaeological contexts of the individuals (burial sites, populations, lifestyles, etc.) and for the storing places (Museum collections, processing labs, research institutions, etc).

The context location (sample ownership/location/archive) can be a complex issue, especially in some countries. For instance in France, the Ministry of Culture provides you with the permits to excavate a site, to import and export bones etc. However, the material we use oftentimes come from the archaeologists' or the anthropologist's own collection (not an 'official' collection/repository per se). Having such information recorded on an open-access public repository may lead to legal repercussions for these sample providers later down the line. Listing a name could be an option but we would then be faced with the issue of 'naming and shaming'. A solution here would be to put the affiliation of the sample provider.
We need a column specific to the archaeological site

The terminology 'full UDG' or 'partial UDG' is too closely related to specific protocols. Hence, if we want to be precise, and given the importance of knowing if damage needs to be trimmed or not, one solution could be to give the DOI of the USER/UDG treatment, though this is hard to maintain over time. Keeping it simple with 'damage has been removed' is also problematic as you would need to provide more information for future studies re-using your data, such as for instance if there has been methylation detection. A suggestion would be to split this entry into two: 'damage_state' vs 'specific_treatment_type'.
The definition of partial user treatment may be ambiguous, given that you could tune USER units + time of exposure

Enrichment is not really suitable here. This could be both UDG and enriched.

Do we here mean 'ethical' or 'legal'? For instance, a permit can be delivered but the study may not have been approved by an ethical committee. An alternative to 'Authority' could be 'Permit provider' or 'Providing authority'. This needs to be specified in the description (i.e. legal and ethical 'authorities' or 'permit provider').

The ethical authority section feels like a catch-all, that should be separated into multiple columns. For instance, where the sample was acquired (e.g. host institution or museum, affiliations of lead researcher on an expedition), where the sample was processed (e.g. ancient DNA lab), etc.

This field is also misleading. The ethical procedures and the regulations that must be respected are two completely different aspects. Respecting regulations and getting permits from pertinent organisms are mandatory steps to be able to work with archaeological, cultural, etc. material, and do not necessarily involve any ethical assessment. Ethics committees on the other hand are specifically dedicated to assess the ethical aspects of research projects and they can confer advice, which may or may not be mandatory for carrying a research project depending on the country and context. Finally, export permits are another instances, that may be independent of the authorisation of local/regulatory authorities, and from the ethical assessment committees.
Specify speciifcally as ethics in terms of e.g. indigenous groups (moral) vs a 'permit authority' (legal) (see new terms for permit authorty proposal)

individuals__radio_14Cdate, to be changed to individuals__raw_radio_14Cdate: The raw C14 value sent by the dating company, raw, untouched, uncalibrated. In other words, the value given by the C14-dating company.

individuals__radio_14Cerror, to be changed to individuals__raw_radio_14Cerror: 
The error of the raw C14 value sent by the dating company, raw, untouched, uncalibrated.

individuals__radio_14Clabcode: 
The code of the measurement provided by the dating company (i.e., this is a unique code, with the following format : AA-90288 / UGA-8660 / D-AMS 030191 / CIRAM-5979).

individuals__date_median to be renamed as individuals__calBP_C14_median_date: 
This is the calibrated median probability calculated by a calibration software on the raw C14 date of the individual. This value can be used in the analyses, and must be the same value that is included in the column individuals__date.

Contextual dates of sites based of some individuals: 
– sites__radio_14C_date, changed to sites__radio_raw_average_14C_date: 
Average of all ABSOLUTE (Raw) and UNCALIBRATED dates available for that site that can be confidently associated with any other individual of the same site. Only archaeologists can provide the information of when this is possible to do. ATTENTION: is it not always the case that individuals of the same sites are synchronous. Very often archaeological sites are diachronic or chronologically asynchronous.
sites__radio_14C_error to be renamed as sites__radio_raw_average_14C_error

sites__calBP14C_median_date: 
This is the calibrated median probability calculated by a calibration software on the average of the uncalibrated C14 dates of the site. This value can be used in the analyses when there is no direct date of the individual (i.e., no value in individuals__calBP_C14_median_date), and in such cases, it must be the same value that is included in the column individuals__date.

individuals__contextual_date: 
When there is not direct date for the individual, nor association date for the site, this column will have any information of the individual’s date that is not derived from a direct or indirect radio C14 date (e.g., date mentioned in a historical record, archaeological information, dates inferred from objects, dates mentioned in a crypt, or just a very vast category, such as, pre-contact, Holocene, etc.).

individuals__date: 
This the column that contains the best information we can give from an individuals date (In order of relevance, this columns will include: individuals__calBP_C14_median_date -> sites__calBP14C_median_date –> individuals__contextual_date)

individuals__datation_method: 
This categoy is defined by precise, fixed categories (Individual_C14, Site_C14, Contextual, Historical record, objects, etc.)

It would be good to have an ENVO definition more tailored for animal or human dental calculus, in which the biome, ecosystem is the oral cavity, which per se constitutes a very well defined environment for microbial communities. The biome in which the person lived is irrelevant since we don’t really know if the environment of the host has any effect in the oral microbiome, and even if it does, it is not directly connected (e.g., like saying that climatic events in antarctica affect the ecosystem in the Amazon). Also, filling a single different value for each individual dental calculus sample in studies combining populations from different regions would turn filling the form extremely challenging and time consuming for very little gain in exchange.
